content.full_text:
March 23, 1976


MS. Susan Chan McCarthy
Graham and James
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, California 94111

Dear Ms. McCarthy:

Your letter of March 10 to Mr. Robert Stern has been referred to
me for response. In addition to responding to the question
raised in your letter, I also will provide you with written
responses to those questions you raised during our recent
telephone conversation.

1. Does participation by counsel at off-the-record conferences
initiated by the hearing officer of the Public Utilities
Commission have the affect of triggering the time test
contained in regulation 18239(e)(1)(D)?

The off-the-record conferences described in your letter
would be considered as time spent in direct communication
with an agency official. If the time spent off the record
in the aggregate exceeds one hour over a two-month period,
then you would be subject to the time test contained in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(2)(D). This conclusion is
not altered by the fact that the conferences are initiated
by the PUC. In a recent opinion, the Commission stated
"...the Political Reform Act, in establishing reporting
requirements relative to attempts to influence administrative
action, does not condition the reporting obligation on how
the attempt was generated." See opinion in the matter of
Robert H. Nida, Associate Counsel, Automobile Club of
Southern California, 2 FPPC Ops. 1 (No. 75-075(A), Jan. 7,
1976).

Ms. Susan Chan McCarthy                    -2-                    March 23, 1976


2. Does time spent by counsel discussing the case with the client
   and witness count towards time spent influencing administrative
   action?

   The time spent in discussion with clients and witnesses while
   a lobbyist, as defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239, is
   influencing administrative action.

3. Does time spent preparing legal briefs or memoranda count
   towards any of the time tests contained in Section 18239(e)?

   No, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(4) states that time spent
   on research (the gathering of information, statistics, studies,
   analyses and the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs,
   memoranda or the preparation of bill analyses) are not
   includable for the purposes of meeting the time tests of
   subsection (e).

4. Is time spent by counsel listening to testimony by other parties
   involved in the hearing before the PUC in which the counsel is
   involved includable in the time tests found in subsection (e)?

   No, time spent by counsel observing a hearing while waiting to
   participate is includable in hours of public testimony. However,
   if an attorney merely observes the proceeding and does not
   participate in any way, the time spent attending the hearing is
   not includable as "hours of public testimony." (2 Cal. Adm.
   Code Section 18239(e)(4))


If I can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact
me.

                                        Sincerely,



                                        PAUL D. SCHAEFFER
                                        Political Reform Analyst

PDS:mak

cc: Kenneth Finney ✓

[Due to the poor quality and reversed/mirrored nature of this document image, I cannot provide an accurate verbatim transcription. The text appears to be printed upside down and backwards, making it illegible. To properly transcribe this document, I would need a clearer, properly oriented image.]

Nan Hambleton
Ken Finney✓
Natalie West
Ted Prim

March 11, 1976

PAUL D. SCHAEFFER

Lobbyist Questions

Sue McCarthy, a San Francisco attorney, has asked for answers to
a number of questions concerning when a lawyer representing a
client before the PUC becomes a lobbyist. Following are the
questions plus my draft answers. I would appreciate your comments
as soon as possible.

QUESTION 1: Do off-the-record conferences initiated by a member
of the Public Utilities Commission have the effect of triggering
the time test contained in 18239(e)(1)(D)?

DRAFT ANSWER: Yes, because such off-the-record conversations
would be considered direct communications, which are not public
testimony, with an official of an agency to whom the public
testimony is directed.

QUESTION 2: Does time spent by counsel discussing the case with
the client and witness count toward any of the time tests?

DRAFT ANSWER: No.

QUESTION 3: Does time spent preparing legal briefs or memoranda
count towards any of the time tests contained in 18239(e)?

DRAFT ANSWER: No, Regulation 18239(e)(3)(B) states that time spent
on research (the gathering of information, statistics, studies,
analyses and the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs, memoranda
or the preparation of bill analyses) are not includable for the
purposes of meeting the time tests of subsection (e).

Natalie West, Ted Prim                -2-                March 11, 1976

Lobbyist Questions


QUESTION 4: Is time spent by counsel listening to testimony by
other parties involved in the hearing in which the counsel is
involved includable in the time tests found in subsection (e).

DRAFT ANSWER: No.

GRAHAM & JAMES
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94111

OTHER OFFICES
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA
LONG BEACH, CALIFORNIA
NEWPORT BEACH, CALIFORNIA
WASHINGTON, D.C.
ANCHORAGE, ALASKA
LONDON
ROME
MILAN

CABLE ADDRESS ALL OFFICES
"CALAGRA"

I.T.T. 470042
R.C.A. 278399
W.U. 340143

TELEPHONE (415) 986-2171

March 10, 1976

Robert Stern, Esq.
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Direct Communication Under
FPPC Reg. §18239(e)(2)(D)
Our File: PROP 9

Dear Mr. Stern:

Attorneys practicing before the Public Utilities
Commission are not required to register as lobbyists within
the meaning of §82039 of the Government Code unless a sub-
stantial or regular portion of their activities for which
they receive economic consideration is for the purpose of
"influencing . . . administrative action". A regulation
adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission on
August 20, 1975, provides certain time tests which must be
met before such an attorney is in fact considered to have
engaged in these activities a substantial or regular portion
of his time. §18239(e)(2)(D) provides that, if the indivi-
dual spends one hour in direct communication which is not
public testimony with officials of the agency to whom the
public testimony is directed, as well as 40 hours engaging
in such public testimony, the substantial or regular test
will have been exceeded.

We are concerned whether certain activities of
attorneys which are normally thought of as involving the
practice of law and not traditional "lobbying" may be con-
sidered to constitute the type of "direct communication" which
is covered by this regulation. We refer specifically to pre-hearing
conferences which attorneys representing clients proceed-
ings before the Public Utilities Commission engage in at the
request of the hearing examiner assigned to the case. These
conferences generally are on the public record; however, cer-
tain examiners have a practice of occasionally going off the
record during the course of the conference for the purpose of

[The image appears to be a very faint or low-contrast document where the text is largely illegible. While there seem to be some words and lines of text scattered throughout the page, I cannot make out the specific content clearly enough to transcribe it accurately according to the rules provided. The document appears to have multiple paragraphs or sections, but the text is too faint or blurry to read with confidence.]

Robert Stern, Esq.
March 10, 1976
Page Two


delineating issues or discussing particular facts in the
presence of counsel for both sides. Over a two-month
period, it is possible that the time off the record with
the examiner may, in the aggregate, exceed one hour. While
the issues or facts discussed off the record are not neces-
sarily ex parte in nature, an element of advocacy is unavoid-
able given the representative role of counsel in these pro-
ceedings.

We respectfully request your informal opinion
confirming our understanding that time spent when the examiner
requires the proceeding to go off the record during these
conferences, where counsel for both sides are present, for
the purpose of discussing matters raised by the examiner or
to clarify issues, is counted as time spent as "hours of
public testimony", rather than as time spent in "direct
communication", on the ground that this type of communication
is not of the type which was intended to be covered by
§18239(e)(2)(D).

Yours truly,

Susan Chan McCarthy
for
GRAHAM & JAMES

In addition to responding to the question raised in your letter, I also will provide you with written responses to those questions you raised during our recent telephone conversation. 1. Does participation by counsel at off-the-record conferences initiated by the hearing officer of the Public Utilities Commission have the affect of triggering the time test contained in regulation 18239(e)(1)(D)? The off-the-record conferences described in your letter would be considered as time spent in direct communication with an agency official. If the time spent off the record in the aggregate exceeds one hour over a two-month period, then you would be subject to the time test contained in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(2)(D). This conclusion is not altered by the fact that the conferences are initiated by the PUC. In a recent opinion, the Commission stated "...the Political Reform Act, in establishing reporting requirements relative to attempts to influence administrative action, does not condition the reporting obligation on how the attempt was generated."
